name: Caer Sidi
description: Caer Sidi, meaning 'Revolving Fortress' or 'Fortress of the Sidhe,' is a mystical otherworld realm in Welsh mythology, prominently featured in the poetry attributed to Taliesin. It is often described as a luminous, rotating castle or island, located either in the sky, under the sea, or within the earth. Caer Sidi is a place of abundance, beauty, and eternal youth, ruled by a powerful figure, sometimes Gwynn ap Nudd or a mysterious queen. It's associated with magic, wisdom, and the treasures of the otherworld. The fortress is incredibly difficult to reach, guarded by powerful enchantments and requiring immense skill and knowledge to navigate. Within its walls reside otherworldly beings, including deities, spirits, and the spirits of the dead. It's a place of great power and mystery, often sought after by heroes seeking knowledge or magical artifacts. The journey to Caer Sidi is fraught with peril, demanding courage and cunning to overcome the challenges that lie in wait. The realm represents a connection to the ancient Celtic beliefs in an afterlife and the ever-present power of the otherworld interwoven with the mortal realm. Its rotating nature suggests the cyclical nature of time and the ever-changing landscape of the spiritual world. The treasures and wisdom held within Caer Sidi are symbolic of the ultimate rewards for those who are brave and worthy enough to seek them.
subtitle: Revolving Fortress of the Otherworld

keyMyths.title: The Spoils of Annwn
keyMyths.description: In the poem 'Preiddeu Annwfn' (The Spoils of Annwn), Arthur leads a raid on Caer Sidi to retrieve a magical cauldron. The poem describes the dangers and mysteries of the otherworld fortress and the few who returned from the perilous journey.

keyMyths.title: Taliesin's Knowledge
keyMyths.description: Taliesin claims to have visited Caer Sidi and gained immense knowledge from its inhabitants. He speaks of the fortress's wonders and the secrets he learned within its walls, highlighting its role as a source of wisdom.

sources.author: John Matthews
sources.work: Taliesin: Shamanism and the Bardic Mysteries in Britain and Ireland
sources.description: An exploration of the figure of Taliesin and the significance of his poetry, including references to Caer Sidi.
